coredata.dc:title: Picomolar-level electrochemical detection of thiocyanate in the saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco using carbon dots doped Fe3O4 nanocomposite embedded on g-C3N4 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Ponnaiah, Sathish Kumar
Prakash, Periakaruppan
Vellaichamy, Balakumar
Paulmony, Tharmaraj
Selvanathan, Rayappan
coredata.dc:description: Thiocyanate (SCN−) is a metabolite of cyanide detoxification with equivalent toxicity. The SCN−, even in trace concentration affects the health adversely. Notwithstanding various methods have been used for SCN− analysis, a fast and simple electrochemical process has been hardly ever tried. Herein a novel material, carbon dots doped Fe3O4 embedded on g-C3N4 nanosheets (CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4) has been synthesized for the electrochemical detection of SCN− level in the saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco. The synthesized material was characterized by FT-IR, HR-TEM, TGA, UV–Vis and XRD analysis. The linearity range exhibited by this sensor is wide (0.001–0.900 μM) and the detection limit is in picomolar. The proposed system can be very well applied for the construction of sensing devices for clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4
Electrochemical sensing
Differential pulse voltammetry
Thiocyanate

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of CDs (a), CDs/Fe3O4 (b), CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (c).
FT-IR spectra of CDs (a), CDs/Fe3O4 (b), CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (c).
XRD patterns of CDs (a), CDs/Fe3O4 (b), CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (c).
HR-TEM images with different magnifications (a–d), SAED pattern of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (e) and EDX image of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (f).
TGA analysis of CDs (a), CDs/Fe3O4 (b), CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (c).

Cyclic voltammetry curve for GCE with different electrodes in the presence of SCN− (10 μM) (a), different addition of SCN− (from 10 to 80 μM) in 0.05 M PBS (pH 6.0) at scan rate 50 mV s−1 (b), and the calibration plot for the linear dependence of SCN− vs I/μA(c).

Cyclic voltammetry curve for CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE with different scan rates (from 50 to 600 mV s−1) in the presence of 10 μM concentration of SCN− (a), the calibration plot for the linear dependence of oxidation and reduction peak current vs square root of scan rate (b), and effect of pH on the peak current (c).

DPV response of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE in 0.05 M PBS (pH = 6.0) with different concentrations of SCN−: 0.001–0.900 μM (a), The calibration plot of current response vs. concentration of SCN− (b).

DPV response of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE to successive addition of 0.10 μM SCN− and 20 fold excess of cations (Li+, Na+, K+ and Ca2+), anions (SO4 2−, NO3 − and Cl−) and organic species such as ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DP) and glucose (Glu) in 0.05 M PBS (pH 6.0).
The schematic representation of the synthesis of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite.
Schematic representation of SCN− sensing using CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE.
Detailed comparison of the electrocatalytic oxidation of SCN− using various electrode materials.
Determination of SCN− levels in various saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco using the proposed CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified electrode.

head.ce:title: Picomolar-level electrochemical detection of thiocyanate in the saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco using carbon dots doped Fe3O4 nanocomposite embedded on g-C3N4 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Madurai, 625 009, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Arul Anandar College, Karumathur, 625 514, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiocyanate (SCN−) is a metabolite of cyanide detoxification with equivalent toxicity. The SCN−, even in trace concentration affects the health adversely. Notwithstanding various methods have been used for SCN− analysis, a fast and simple electrochemical process has been hardly ever tried. Herein a novel material, carbon dots doped Fe3O4 embedded on g-C3N4 nanosheets (CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4) has been synthesized for the electrochemical detection of SCN− level in the saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco. The synthesized material was characterized by FT-IR, HR-TEM, TGA, UV–Vis and XRD analysis. The linearity range exhibited by this sensor is wide (0.001–0.900 μM) and the detection limit is in picomolar. The proposed system can be very well applied for the construction of sensing devices for clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiocyanate (SCN−) concentration is used as a biomarker for environmental cyanide exposure [1–3]. Even the trace level of SCN− exerts adverse cardiovascular threats, increases the menace of cancer, and affects the protein dialysis and secretion of iodine by thyroid glands in the human body [4–6]. Hence an appropriate method is required for the detection of SCN− which should be applied in forensic investigation and medical diagnosis. Until now many techniques have been used for the determination of SCN−, which include colorimetric analysis [4], flow spectrophotometric [7], fluorescent spectroscopy [8], gas chromatography [9], and ion chromatography [10]. Despite these many analytical methods for SCN− analysis, a fast and simple electrochemical method for analyzing a large number of samples has been very rarely addressed. Herein we report an accurate, sensitive and simple electrochemical process for the detection of trace SCN− (10ˆ˗12 M).

An exhaustive research has been focused in recent years on carbon based materials with unique properties for several applications [11,12]. Particularly the carbon dots (CDs) are used in electrochemical studies due to their biocompatibility, chemical inertness, electronic conductivity and porosity. But what limits their applications in electrochemical field is their difficult immobilization on electrode surface [13]. To dodge this problem, the researchers usually binarize the CDs with transition metal oxides [14,15]. Again the binary component poses problems such as poor sensitivity, selectivity and stability [16]. Hence supporting constituents such as carbon/nitrogen based materials are always required. In general, g-C3N4 nanosheets expose all their high surface area to guest species, which show greater potential compared to single and binary components. So far there is no report at all about the fabrication of electrochemical sensors for the detection of SCN− based on g-C3N4. In the present work, a facile and simple synthesis of CDs doped Fe3O4 embedded on g-C3N4 nanosheets has been proposed. The as-prepared CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 composite was characterized by FT-IR, HR-TEM, TGA, UV–Vis and XRD analysis. The feasibility and validation of the proposed sensor have been done in real samples by detecting SCN− in the saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco.

Melamine was purchased from Aldrich. Citric acid (CA), Fe (NO3)3 and FeCl3 were of analytical reagent grade and purchased from Merck. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH were prepared using 0.05 M solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. All the solutions were prepared using double distilled water.

When 2.0 g of CA was heated to 200 °C for 5 min, liquated CA was obtained. Later, the colorless liquated CA became pale yellow and finally after 30 min, CA in orange color was obtained. These color changes confirm the formation of CDs. The CDs with pH 7.0 was obtained by adding NaOH (100 mL of 10 mg mL−1) under vigorous stirring. Then the colloidal solution of CDs was stored in an amber colored bottle in the refrigerator. The g-C3N4 was prepared according to the literature [17] by thermal poly condensation of melamine. The ternary nanocomposite, CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 was prepared in a typical procedure: 0.5 mL of CDs solution was mixed with 0.2 g of g-C3N4 and 0.5 M each of Fe(NO3)3 and FeCl3. The resulting solution was added into ammonia solution. .It was sonicated for about 2 h. Finally the suspension was dried at 80 °C overnight and then calcined with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 3 h. The same procedure was followed in the absence of g-C3N4 to prepare binary CDs/Fe3O4. The overall process of formation of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 is shown in Scheme 1 .

Electrochemical experiments were performed using a three-electrode cell (Model 660E, Austin, TX, USA) under nitrogen atmosphere at room temperature. The morphology of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 was studied by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI TECNAI T20 G2). TGA was carried out with TGA-50, HIMADZU. For XRD analysis, Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418oA) on JEOL JDX 8030 X-ray diffractometer was used. FT-IR study used JASCO FT-IR 460 Plus spectrophotometer. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Jasco (V-560) model.

GCE was initially polished with 1, 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina powder successively and washed ultrasonically in water, ethanol, and acetone. 2 mg of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite was dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol with sonication for a few minutes to yield a stable suspension. 5 μL of the resultant solution was coated onto the surface of the GCE. For comparison, several electrodes including bare GCE, CDs, CDs/Fe3O4 and CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 were prepared, and their electrochemical performances were investigated towards SCN−.

The absorbance peak of the CDs appears at 318 nm resulting from π-π* transition which is shown in Fig. 1 (a). When CDs are doped with Fe3O4, a shoulder band is observed at the same wavelength (Fig. 1(b)), which indicates the formation of CDs/Fe3O4. As seen in Fig. 1(c), for CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite, a broad band is observed at 250–310 nm. These results reveal that the CDs and Fe3O4 are embedded on g-C3N4 surface which may be via physisorption, electrostatic binding or charge-transfer interactions [17].

From the FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2 (a)), the presence of amide functional groups is confirmed, as evidenced by their vibrational frequencies centered at 730-768 cm−1 (aromatic CH bond), 1260 cm−1 (COC stretching vibrations), 1555 cm−1 (NH in-plane bending), 1648 cm−1 (C＝O stretching bond), 2926 cm−1 (CH vibration) and 3457 cm−1 (OH stretching vibration of the carboxylic group). The spectrum of CDs/Fe3O4 (Fig. 2(b)) exhibits characteristic peaks at 449-680 cm−1 which can be attributed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of FeO. That the peaks are slightly shifted to a higher wavenumber after placing Fe3O4 indicates the interaction between Fe3O4 and CDs [18]. For the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite, the peak appearing at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to C–N stretching vibration mode, while the peaks at 1242, 1320, and 1405 cm−1 are due to aromatic C–N stretching. The out-of-plane bending modes of triazine rings show bands near 808 and 646 cm−1. The broad peaks at around 2906–3420 cm−1 come from stretching vibration modes of –NH and hydroxyl of the adsorbed H2O as presented in Fig. 2(c). It is worth noting that all of the above characteristic peaks in the spectrum of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 demonstrate the successful assembly of CDs/Fe3O4 onto the surface of g-C3N4 [17].

The crystallography of the nanocomposite, CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 was studied by XRD analysis. The pattern for CDs is presented in Fig. 3 (a) which shows a wider (0 0 2) peak centered at 24° which belongs to the turbostratic carbon phase. The pattern for CDs/Fe3O4 demonstrates some sharp diffraction peaks appearing at around 18.6°, 30.2°, 35.6°, 46.9°, 53.1°, 56.6°, 62.4°, 66.4°, 70.4°, 74.4° and 78.0°, which can be indexed to the crystal planes of (1 1 1), (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1), (4 4 0), (5 3 1), (6 2 0), (6 2 2) and (4 4 4) respectively of Fe3O4 (JCPDS 33-0664). An additional peak is also observed at 2θ = 24.23 as shown in Fig. 3(b), which corresponds to the CDs [19]. The width of (3 1 1) peak was engaged to compute the average crystalline size of the CDs/Fe3O4, which is found to be 31 nm. Moreover, the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 composite presents XRD pattern similar to that of CDs/Fe3O4 and two distinct peaks are now found at 27.4° and 13.1°, which correspond to the characteristic inter-layer structural packing of g-C3N4 and inter planar staking of aromatic systems as exposed in Fig. 3(c). These XRD results confirm the successful assembly of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4.

The g-C3N4 exhibits characteristic nanosheets structure with a smooth surface in white color, and lot of regular CDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles are dispersed well on the surface of g-C3N4 as indicated in Fig. 4 (a) [20]. The black color particles in spherical shape specify CDs/Fe3O4 which are embedded on the g-C3N4 nanosheets as shown in Fig. 4(b–d). These images demonstrate that the size of Fe3O4 and CDs are less than 28 nm and 5 nm as evidenced from Fig. 4(c & d). The crystallinity of the nanocomposite was observed by SAED pattern as shown in Fig. 4(e). The bright circular rings corresponding to (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) planes confirm the crystallinity of nanocomposite. The composition of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 was ascertained from EDX analysis as presented in Fig. 4(f). The elemental composition in weight percent and atomic percent units in the inspection field is shown in Fig. 4(f) (inset).

TGA of plain CDs is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The early weight loss of 8–10% for the CDs observed between 130 and 200 °C is attributed to the water molecules loss and later a steady loss in weight is observed up to 500 °C owing to the loss of other species which might be attached through weak hydrogen interactions. As shown in Fig. 5(b), after binarization (CDs/Fe3O4), there is a decrease in weight loss from 70% to 36%, which is due to the presence of Fe3O4. In the case of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4, the decomposition starts initially at around 30–100 °C which corresponds to the exclusion of physically adsorbed water molecules, and the main decomposition occurs at 480–670 °C due to the degradation of g-C3N4 and CDs. Thus the TGA data clearly reveal that the low thermal stability of g-C3N4 and CDs is greatly enhanced due to the presence of thermally stable Fe3O4 material as shown in Fig. 5(c).

In a conventional three-electrode setup, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements studied the electrocatalytic behavior of modified electrode under PBS (0.05 M, pH 7.0). Fig. 6 (a) displays the CVs of bare GCE, CDs/GCE, CDs/Fe3O4/GCE and CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE electrodes with the addition of 10 μM SCN−. The response signals of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE towards SCN− specify a strong electro-oxidation at 1.3 V [3]. The peak current of the electrodes increases in the order: bare GCE < CDs/GCE < CDs/Fe3O4/GCE < CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE, which reveals an enhanced oxidation of SCN− at the surface of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE. This enhanced electrochemical performance arises due to the interactions between the inter-constituents namely CDs, Fe3O4, and g-C3N4 resulting in the improvement of the high electron transfer [17]. The electrocatalytic behavior of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE hybrid towards the oxidation of SCN− was further investigated by varying the SCN− concentration. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the sensing signal linearly increases with the concentration of SCN− in the range of 10–80 μM. The oxidation current at 1.3 V exhibits a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9977) with the SCN− concentration, indicating the sensitivity of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (Fig. 6(c)). Moreover, the anodic peak currents (Ia) increases with the increase of the square root of scan rate over the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE with the shifts from negative to positive direction, which implies that the electron transfer is quasi-reversible (Fig. 7 (a)). That the function of the square root of scan rates with peak current gives a linear coefficient of 0.9929 (Fig. 7(b)) indicates the electrochemical reaction at the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4/GCE as a diffusion-controlled process [21].

The electro-oxidation of SCN− at CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE was investigated by CV with different phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) and the results are shown in Fig. 7(c). When the pH of the solution increases from 2.0 to 10.0, the maximum peak current is attained at pH 6.0. In basic conditions, the hydroxide ions interfere and generate oxide layers on the surface of the electrode, and in acidic conditions, the protonation of SCN− takes place resulting in the reduction of current response [3]. Hence, pH 6.0 PBS was used in the subsequent experiments.

The DPV was employed in 0.05 M PBS by varying the SCN− concentration and applied potential of 0.2–1.3 V at the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE. In the DPV responses of SCN− from low concentration to high concentration (0.001 μM- 0.900 μM), the oxidation peak current at 1.02 V exhibits a remarkable linear enhancement (Fig. 8 (a)), which suggests that the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE can be applied for the quantitative determination of SCN−. Moreover when increasing the concentrations, the oxidation peak potentials are slightly shifted to the positive potential and this shift is directly proportional to the concentration of SCN−. The reason for the peak shift is that a high concentration of SCN− at the interface of the electrode–electrolyte makes a thin film formation or more adsorption at the electrode surface [21]. The oxidation peak current (Ipa) of SCN− has a good linear relationship with the concentration of SCN− in the range from 0.001 to 0.900 μM (Fig. 8(b)) with the correlation coefficient of 0.9897. The parameter, limit of detection (LOD) stipulates the minimum detectable amount of analyte using the developed sensor. The LOD is calculated using formula [22].
LOD=3 .3S/ b
where S is the standard deviation of the lowest concentration of SCN−, and b is the slope of calibration curve obtained from the DPV. The detection limit is found to be 140 pM which is very much lower than the corresponding values reported in the literature. The proposed CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite has an excellent sensing ability, lower LOD and good linear relationship as illustrated in Table 1 [3,23–27]. The possible electron transfer mechanism at CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite is shown in Scheme 2 .

A series of six repetitive measurements were carried out for a fixed concentration of 50 μM SCN− after a storing period of 21 days at 4 °C and the peak current decrease of the electrode is found to be just less than 4 ± 0.03%. The CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified electrode shows a very good response with a relative standard deviation of 2.2% even after 80 successive cycles. These results indicate that the modified electrode possesses an excellent reproducibility, repeatability and stability.

The influence of interference on the detection of SCN− anion was evaluated in the presence of various ions. Three order or four order higher concentrations of different cations such as Li+, Na+, K+ and Ca2+, anions such as, SO4 2−, NO3 − and Cl− and organic species such as ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DP) and glucose (Glu) were added and analyzed. From Fig. 9 , it can be clearly seen that these substances do not interfere in the electro-oxidation. The major advantage of the CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 modified GCE is its practicality for the detection of SCN− in the real samples without being influenced by any interference.

Finally, the proposed sensor material was applied to evaluate the practical applicability of CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 in a series of the saliva samples of smokers and non-smokers of tobacco. 1.0 mL of saliva samples of smokers who smoke 2 cigarettes a day (35 age), 5 cigarettes a day (50 age), 1 packet (10 nos. a day) (70 age), and non-smokers (in the age group of 35, 50 and 70) and the samples were diluted 100 times with PBS (pH 6.0). The standard addition experiments were carried out with three spiked levels of SCN− (0.5 μM) and the results are shown in Table 2 . Favorable recoveries are able to be obtained, thereby indicating the reliability of the proposed sensor for real sample analysis. The results show that the level of SCN− in the saliva samples of smokers of tobacco is higher than that of non-smokers.

A novel CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 nanocomposite has been successfully synthesized and used as an efficient electrochemical sensing platform for SCN−. The proposed material exhibits high sensitivity, low detection limit, high stability and wide detection range. The high-performance achieved by CDs/Fe3O4@g-C3N4 based SCN− sensor shows a great potential for the detection of trace SCN− (pM) in the saliva samples of smokers and nonsmokers of tobacco. This work can be efficaciously used for the fabrication of sensing devices which may provide valuable information in clinical diagnosis.
